description: A14FJ - Day 4, part 5: Lunar Orbit Insertion and First Impressions

tokens: Both Mission Control and the crew of Apollo 14 are counting minutes until the time the large SPS engine at the rear of the CSM ignites and puts the combined CSM-LM stack into a lunar orbit.
With the burn taking place behind the Moon, the Apollo crew trio will be out of radio contact, leaving very nervous controllers waiting for the good news to come.
Although the crew are surely keen to observe the Moon up close for the first time, their priority has to be in executing their manoeuvres perfectly.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We have had Loss of Signal as Apollo 14 went around the corner of the Moon for the first time.
Coming up some 12 minutes from now on lunar orbit insertion burn to recapitulate some of the numbers associated with this maneuver.
The ignition time is 82 hours 36 minutes 46 seconds for burn time on the big engine, theSPSengine of 6 minutes 12 seconds for a total velocity change in retrograde of 3022 feet per second [921 m/s].
Assuming a normal burn on time the spacecraft should be reacquired as it comes around the other side of the Moon by the ground station at 82 hours 56 minutes 27 seconds.

Among those people in the viewing room behind mission control room here are acting NASA Administrator, George Low, Associated Administrator from Manned Space Flight, Dale Myers and Manned Spacecraft Center Director, Dr.
Robert R.
Gilruth.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Coming up on 11 minutes even until ignition forLOI.
Mark.
11 minutes until ignition Lunar Orbit Insertion burn.
At 82 hours 25 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: While Apollo 14 slips beyond radio contact, the crew has turned on the DSE tape recorder.
We will be able to follow their conversation while the ship swings behind the Moon and towards the point of LOI burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Sitting on the righthand seat, Ed is positioned to flip the MAIN BUS TIE switches that connect the batteries to the two DC power distribution buses.
Having the batteries online supplements the fuel cells during the high power demand of the SPS burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The AVERAGEG routine in the computer updates the state vector every 2 seconds whenever the spacecraft is in powered flight, ie.
has engines firing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Both SPS banks, A and B, are now running.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: One of the FDAI 8-balls has been switched to show their attitude based on data derived from the two BMAG gyroscope assemblies that provide backup attitude information via the GDC, or Gyro Display Coupler.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: BEGIN LUNAR REV 1.
transcript.speaker: Note

tokens: Apollo 14 is now in lunar orbit.
Their first run around the Moon will be known as Rev 1, or Revolution 1.
During the intense work up to the first lunar landing in 1969, the question of how to count revolutions around the Moon arose.
It was decided to use the 180th meridian, or the longitude, at the far side of the Moon - which is also incidentally very close to the spot, relative to the surface, where the LOI burn takes place.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stu is commenting that the burn is going exactly as it did in the Command Module Simulator.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Tsiolkovsky is a remarkable formation on the farside of the Moon.
It is a crater some 184 kilometers in diameter, with a prominent central peak and a dark mare-like surface due to magma having filled the crater at some point during its history.
It is named after the Russian rocketry pioneer Konstantin Tsiolkovsky.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, 82 hours 55 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
About 52 seconds away from acquisition of signal here as Apollo 14 comes around the Moon on it's first lunar orbit.
We're standing by as we await word of lockup with the signal from the spacecraft by the stations in the manned space flight tracking network.
30 seconds, 20 seconds, they've hadAOSin Honeysuckle.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, Apollo 14.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Go ahead, 14.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, Freddo.
We had an extremely fine burn, the burn report as follows: burn time, 6 plus 11; residuals, 0.3 plus zero, and zero;Delta-Vc, minus 7.3; 0351; oxidizer, 349.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, 14.
We copied.
Wonder if you can give us a reading on the unbalance.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay.
It was decrease 40, 4 0.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Roger, Al.
Decrease 4 0.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Roger.
This is really a wild place up here.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Hey, Ron, you're not going to believe this.
It looks just like the map.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Hey, that's great, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-080
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 14.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, Freddo; you might tell Ron there that, at this Sun angle, things look real good.
Picked up that landmark 1 on the zero phase.
Our zero phase target, no sweat, and landmark 2, I got the A part of that one, at least.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Hey, Stu, that sounds great, by golly.
It looks like our orbit - orbital science is off to a good start then.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes.
For now, anyway.
I think we'll probably get better as time goes on, but it really looks - it really looks great as far as picking up all the targets that we looked at so long.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 14.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Go ahead, 14.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Well, this really is a wild place up here.
It has all of the grays, browns, whites, dark craters that everybody's talked about before.
It's really quite a sight.
Ed and Stu are looking at the landmarks to the map, picking up landmarks very easily as we go along the way.
And, of course, in the type of orbit we are, the fact that we're climbing away from the surface is very obvious.
Of course, that brings more and more of the area in detail.
It really is quite a sight.
No atmosphere at all; everything is clear up here.
Really fantastic.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Although by any practical definition, vacuum-like conditions do exist on the Moon, an extremely tenuous atmosphere of noble gases such as neon, argon and helium, as well as hydrogen, does exist.

Measuring its exact composition was a great challenge, considering that the exhaust from a single lunar landing deposited enough gas on the lunar surface to practically double the mass of the lunar atmosphere.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Al.
We copied, and I think you covered all bets with the colors there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Al, I expect that you guys ...
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That's because they're all here, Freddo.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Al, I expected you to invent a new one on this flight.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Give us - give us a little time.
We'll think up something.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: 14, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Go ahead, Freddo.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Ed, can you verify the Flow Valve is in Increase now?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That's verified.
It's in Increase.
And I put it in Increase about 50 seconds before the end of the burn, and left it there.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Freddo, the - I think the best description - the description that comes to my mind, we mentioned this when we first looked at this thing, is that it looks like a plaster mold that somebody has dusted with grays and browns, but it looks like it's been molded out of plaster of paris.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 83 hours 8 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Our display here in Mission Control shows that Apollo 14's present orbit is 58.4 pericynthion by 168.6 apocynthion.
Continuing to monitor air ground.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 14, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay.
TheS-IVBimpact should occur in about 8 minutes.
We'll give you the word on that, when it happens.
And the first look at trajectory says you're in a 169.6 by 58.9.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, Freddo.
We copy that.

And looking out of my rendezvous window now, we've just passed over Sklodowska, and those rays coming out of that bright crater that Ron and I talked about - stand out a lot better than what I expected to see them, Ron.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The crater Sklodowska and its satellite craters are named after the Polish physicist and chemist, double Nobel laureate Marie Sklodowska (Curie.)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: He's listening.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Freddo, I copy 168.6 by 56.9.
Is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: No.
The numbers were 169.6 by 58.9.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay.
Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: And, Freddo, you might pass on to Ron there, while he's listening, that I was on the wrong side of the cockpit there for old King.
I couldn't tell anything about the rays coming down from Bruno.
Maybe next time around.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Crater Bruno is named after the Italian astronomer Giardano Bruno (1548-1600)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, we're just now passing over Ansgarius and La Prouse.
The cone in Behaim is more rounded, I guess, than what I expected - expected to see.
[Garble], you know, was talking about it being large and quite rounded.
It is that - it's a little more subdued than what I expected to see.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Ansgarius and its satellite craters are named after Saint Ansgar, a German theologian.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crater La Prouse and its satellites are named after Jean Francois de Galoup (1741-1788) Comte La Prouse, a French explorer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Behaim and its satellites are named after the German cartographer, Martin Behaim.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Stu.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And I can see off to my right here Humboldt coming up, and it's just as impressive as it is in the pictures.
One thing that strikes me about it is, the dark areas aren't quite as dark, I guess, as I thought, but we're moving in with a reasonably high Sun here, so they're kind of washing out.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Crater Humboldt is named after the German philologist Wilhelm von Humboldt.
He should not be confused with his brother Alexander (1769-1859) whose namesame on the lunar surface is Mare Humboldtianum.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, 14; Houston.
Hate to pull you away from the window, but I got a map update and a flight-plan update when you get a chance.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, on the map update, Rev 2, 180 degrees at 84:44:53.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, I show 084:44:53 for Rev 2 on the map.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Roger.
That's correct, Al, and in the Flight Plan at 88:30 - [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, change theLTCattitude to roll, 352; pitch, 146; yaw, 355.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay,LTCattitude 88:30, 352, 146, 355.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, and the reason for the big change is the other angles were forSEFand they should be forBEF, and that's what I just gave you.
Next change is at 89:08.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, High Gain angles should be Pitch, minus 46; Yaw, 194.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, I show minus 46 and 194.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, that's it, Al.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Thank you, Freddo.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Freddo, as interesting as this is from orbit, it sure makes one eager to get - to want to get at Cone Crater now.
I'll bet it's wild down there.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Cone Crater is both the prominent landmark and main goal of their forthcoming landing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, I missed your first there, Ed.
What'd you say about Cone Crater?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I said, as interesting as this is from orbit, all it does is whet your appetite to get to Cone Crater.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I guess in 3 days I haven't brainwashed him where the real science is done, Freddo.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: His arms aren't long enough to bring him back from there.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, you can really get the attitude rate as we're climbing up here to apogee, Houston, and we're climbing right up with Langrenus there.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Roger, Stu.
And we just hadS-IVBimpact.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And we're - you get the feeling here, climbing up, that we're almost going up vertically here over Langrenus.
It's staying right in the window and that rooster tail coming out of Petavius B is very evident as it swings up across and passes up by Langrenus.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Roger, Stu.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: From this particular location, looking out the window number 5, you can see the upper end of the Sea of Fertility, and it's interesting that the only way I can tell the demarc - line of demarcation is an albedo change.

At this moment, I can't tell you what causes the albedo change except - it's not apparent - except that the Sea of Fertility is much darker and then it changes into the much lighter region up to the north.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Say, Ron, old Goclenius looks just like the picture.
You can see the rille going right across the rim and stretching on up; [Garble] did his job well.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 14, do you read Houston?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Go ahead, Houston; you're loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay.
Just radio check, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We lost our displays down here, and just want to make sure we hadn't lost anything else.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: [Garble] - okay [Garble] -
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Al's trying to get us to get on with it; we're trying to gawk at the same time.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, we're just starting to pick up the edge of Nectaris now, coming across with the - can't quite see up far enough to - oh, yes, there's ole Daguerre 66, just like - just like a neon sign out there.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Mare Nectaris
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Daguerre is named after Louis Daguerre (1789-1851), the French inventor of photography.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Stu, and we're looking at the tracers on the Apollo 12 seismometer, and it's essentially still looking undamped.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Still vibrating ...
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: [Garble] Great.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Hello, Freddo.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yes, that's right, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Is it essentially like neutral stability, Fred, or they coming down on it?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: It looks like, if anything, Stu, maybe they're still building a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Ah so.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: There's Theophilus, Stu, there at the top of the window; can you see it?.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You know, Fred, I get the impression that, just from looking at the real thing, these craters are a lot fresher than the photographs led me to believe.
I'm very surprised at how fresh many of them look.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've just been remarking up here, Freddo, how easy it is to find these landmarks.
They just stand right out for you.
It's really magnificent.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Let's hope Cone and Triplet and Doublet show up the same way.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: All tree crater units mentioned are landmarks at the immediate Fra Mauro landing area, and extremely familiar for the fellow LMPs Fred Haise and Ed Mitchell who both rained for landing there.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You're right.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Freddo, my first impression as I - How do you read, Fred?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I read you loud and clear, Stu.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay.
First impression of the Theophilus and Cyrillus is - much difference in age than what I expected.

Of course, Theophilus being the younger, but it's much more so than what I really expected - to see, and we hope to get some good photos of the line between the two there; but it's pretty amazing how much more subdued Cyrillus is.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The two craters are located side by side and share part of their rim.
Theophilus is younger, with sharper features and a prominent central peak that is not been eroded by billions of years of meteoroid bombardment.
Cyrillus is clearly much older and consequently more heavily eroded.
Its northeastern rim has has been obliterated by Theophilus and most of its features have been softened by the younger crater's ejecta blanket which has been draped over it.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, we copied that, Stu.
And, we're up with you on the chart now, too.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay.
And we're just approaching Descartes now, Freddo.
I'm looking out of my rendezvous window, and four of those too-bright craters on either side of the landing site.
Just - it's so plain.
I guess I'm surprised at the detail - that you can see.
Of course, the Sun angle is dropping down a little here, but I thought probably at Descartes, the two bright lead-ins - but - you can see many, many of the fine - the fine craters in between them.
And, of course, Dollond, Dollond E, and Kant, they're all just very vivid.
But, the - the detail you can see from this altitude of the Descartes sites are rather amazing.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The crater Dollond and its satellite craters are named after John Dollond (1706-1761, the British optician and master telescope maker.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crater Kant is named after the German philosopher Immanuel Kant (1724-1801).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Stu.
I hope theLTCcaptures the same detail, a little greater maybe.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Roger.
And it'll he a little lower, too.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Freddo, I - At this point, we're start to pick up a few shadows on the edges of the craters, but the crater chain coming out of Abulfeda - really can't see much difference in albedo between the crater itself and a good bit of the surrounding territory.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Albedo is the term to describe a surface's ability to reflect incoming light.
In Stu's opinion, the crater Abulfeda and the surrounding area appear to be uniform in this respect.
It all looks the same to him in terms of surface lightness.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
We're crossing over Albategnius and we can look up and see Ptolemaeus; yes, it's coming in to the hatch window now.
That's very stark.
I'm very impressed by the different - by albedo differences here, Freddo, that I - that I assume from photographs are really more Sun angle problems or photographic problems.
But, by golly, there's some that are really here that I never expected.
They must indicate contacts of some sort that may be very subtle on the surface; but I actually believe they're there, if one can find them.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Crater Albategnius is named for Abū Abd Allāh Muḥammad ibn Jābir ibn Sinān al-Raqqī al-Ḥarrānī aṣ-Ṣābial-Battānī, a Medieval Arab astronomer whose name was Latinized as Albategnius.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Ptolemaeus is named after Claudius Ptolemy, the ancient Roman astronomer and polymath.
His major work on astronomy is the Almagest, which survived into the Middle ages in Arabic form, before being rediscovered in the western world.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Ed.
Do you mean - you talking in terms of large scale or small scale, like within crater floor?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: No, I'm thinking of a little larger scale than that.
But there are some in the crater floors that are very surprising, too.
There's so many things that I assumed from maps were more photographic peculiarities rather than real life, but, by golly, I think they're here.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I think, given time, we could find flows and different units that may very well be covered with a great deal of regolith that we - we're seeing suggestions of them in albedo differences and textural differences that are clearly visible from this altitude.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I've noticed some lineations in some of the - the big craters that are suggestive of - well, I'm not sure it's of the sloping type of circular lineations that you see in craters.
I guess, to be safe, I ought to guess that - so I'll be safe and not say any more.
I did see lineations that were surprising to me.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: These close to the wall, Ed, or down within the floor of the crater itself?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Well, all up and down the wall of a few craters; I don't recall which ones now - back along our track here a few miles.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Looking down at the Crater Herschel, for example, Freddo, on the - Let's see, on the western wall.
To me, it appears as though it's really a solid chunk from - the rim down to the first slope, or the - where it breaks slope.
Of course, this is a long ways away to be talk - making talk like that.
However, it seems - it gives one the impression of being very hard solid rock [Garble.] [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, we copied, Ed.
Herschel's west wall looks like essentially one unit.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That's affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The Herschel crater is named after Frederick William Herschel (1738-1822), the German-British astronomer best known for the discovering the planet Uranus.
Both his son John and his sister Caroline - astronomers in their own right - have namesake craters on the Moon as well.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, 14; Houston.
The 12 seismometers are still going.
The traces right now look maybe to be almost neutral.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, Freddo.

We just passed over Mösting A, which we'll get a better look at next time around, and the Davy crater chain stands out real vividly and just starting to get a pretty low Sun angle here, so any albedo differences about those will be hard to tell now.
You can see the dark halo spots along the rilles in Alphonsus, however, very vividly.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Roger, Stu.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: As we approach the terminator, Freddo, with some fairly high crater walls and high country - with these long shadows, it really looks rugged.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes.
Guess right about the terminator; you're starting to look into the eastern edge of the Fra Mauro formation.
Is that what it looks like, Ed?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, that's it.
That's affirmative, Freddo.
Unfortunately, the landing site, I think, is definitely in darkness.
It's probably a little bit too far to the south for us to even see that area too well.
A crater, Gambart, we can just see the - the Gambart - we can just see the eastern rim of it.
The western rim is just barely lighted, but the rest of it's in darkness.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Crater Gambart is located almost directly north of Fra Mauro.
It is named after Jean-Flix Adolphe Gambart, (1800-1836), the French astronomer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Of course, this Sun angle makes it pretty obvious [Garble] deposit of some kind.
You can see the difference in texture very easily from a low Sun angle.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Roger, Al.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That's affirmative.
You can see the streaks - the streaks that we've talked about are really there.
They seem to lead right back toward the Imbrium area and the Copernican area.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: That's the most stark - desolate-looking piece of country I've ever seen.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're almost directly over the terminator now, Freddo.
And looking right down into these craters and these features that are right on the terminator.
And even knowing it, it is a terminator, and knowing what you're supposed to see, it's very difficult to make out exactly what you are looking at.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: With our current dark adaptation, it looks like you could walk along that surface into the darkness and fall into nothing.
There's absolutely nothing there.
Perhaps when we are some - a little better dark adapted, you see Earth - features in Earthshine, but we can't right now.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Hey, Freddo, we sure picked a clear day to arrive; there's not much haze in the air at all.
We can see all the way to the horizon.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Incredible.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Stu and Fred are joking, of course.
The Moon's exceedingly thin atmosphere would not be able to cause any perceivable visual phenomena.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I guess we better make the boss happy and get on with the chow.
[Garble] knock off our descriptions at this point.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, Ed, you'll get another chance to look at it a couple of REVs later, a little lower.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Roger.
There sure is a lot to see.
You could spend a lot of time talking about it.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-081
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: While the crew isnt being chatty, they continue to work.
One task performed is Stu's 10th P52 realignment of the guidance platform during the flight.
As a reference, he sighted on star 30 (Menkent) and star 35 (Rasalhague).
As a check of his sighting accuracy, the computer compared the measured angle between these stars and the actual angle.
The difference between them was 000.01 degrees, a nearly perfect result.
Finally, the computer displayed Noun 93, the angles by which the gimbals would be rotated or 'torqued' to restore perfect alignment.
These were 0.001 ° in X, -0.055° in Y and 0.002° in Z axis.
According to the post-flight Mission Report, the platform was torqued at 084:10 GET.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: After using the PTC REFSMMAT since 013:13 GET for his alignments, at this time, the Apollo guidance system is realigned to the landing site orientation REFSMMAT.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, Apollo 14.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Apollo 14, Houston.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Oh, hello, Gordon.
Any words for us before we go over the hill?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yes, sir.
Just getting ready to call you.
The Orange Team is now at your service.
And I have a change - slight change, to theTEI-4 pad.
If you'll get that one out, I'll give them - give you the change numbers.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Hey, Gordon, I'm having an orange drink in favor - in honor of the Orange Team.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Very good.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Ed, if you're ready to copy, we have about 1 minute to get this in.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'll just read the change numbers.
The weight changed slightly, new weight is 38230; new Tig.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Very long comm break.
